project.title: Palaeoproteomics of archaeological shell beads
project.projectDescription: In this project we have applied palaeoproteomics to study archaeological shell beads dating back to the beginning of the 7th mill. cal BCE and recovered from a prehistoric site in Jordan (Ba'ja). These beads were made of mollusc shells and were of unknown origin, but suspected to be made of larger bivalves such as Tridacna or Spondylus sp. The aim of the study was to analyse ancient proteins extracted from these beads in order to infer their biological origin. Tiny amounts of ancient samples were used and the extracted proteins were analysed by high resolution liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS). In order to identify obtained peptides, we have created an Ã¢â‚¬Ëœin-houseÃ¢â‚¬â„¢ molluscan protein database, by gathering molluscan sequences (proteins and transcriptomes), which were downloaded from publicly available databases in NCBI. Searching against this comprehensive database, we were able to identify a number of shell proteins from Tridacna sp., the giant clam shells. Our study reports the oldest molluscan protein sequences ever recovered that come from a very warm environment.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Three archaeological samples were grinded with a micropestle directly in an eppendorf to obtain a fine powder. To obtain the intracrystalline protein fraction, the samples were bleached by adding concentrated NaOCl (6-14%) and left for 48 h; a blank sample was also prepared. Bleach was removed by centrifugation (10 min, 15k rpm), samples were rinsed with ultrapure water (5x times) and ethanol (u.p. grade 1x time). Powders were demineralised with EDTA solution (0.5 M, Sigma-Aldrich, pH=8) in a shaker (1500 rpm, RT) using a ratio of 1:20 (mg/ÃŽÂ¼L, powder in solution). The demineralised samples were reduced using 1M DL-Dithiothreitol (Sigma) for 1h at 65 Ã‚Â°C and alkylated with 0.5 M iodoacetamide at RT in the dark. The samples were centrifuged (10 min, 15k rpm) to separate soluble and insoluble fractions. The two fractions were treated separately. 1) The insoluble fraction was digested with the enzyme trypsin. 2) The soluble fraction was processed without digestion. The different fractions were recombined at the peptide clean-up step. In detail: 1) to all the insoluble aliquots, 100 ÃŽÂ¼L of Ambic solution was added (50 mM, pH 7.5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ8) and digestion carried out with 1 ÃŽÂ¼L of trypsin (0.4 ÃŽÂ¼g/ÃŽÂ¼L, Promega, proteomics grade), overnight (~16 hrs). Digestion was stopped with 1 ÃŽÂ¼L of 10% TFA. The digests were further acidified with 10 % TFA to reach pH Ã¢â€°Ë†2 before the peptide clean-up with C18 stage tips. 2) The soluble aliquots were directly acidified to lower down the pH to Ã¢â€°Ë†3-4 using TFA (10% and 50%). The peptides obtained from the soluble and insoluble fractions were purified and combined using in-house made C18 stage-tips (Rappsilber et al. 2007). The C18 tips were equilibrated and washed according to standard protocol. Eluted twice with 20 ÃŽÂ¼L of each 60% ACN and 40% ACN. The eluate was concentrated in a vacuum centrifuge before LC-MS/MS analysis.  The remaining eluate was resuspended with 0.1% TFA and 5% ACN solution and injected  an EASY-nLC 1200 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bremen, Germany) coupled to a Exploris  orbitrap mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bremen, Germany) on a 77 min gradient. Chromatographic and MS parameters were then performed based on previously published methods for ancient and degraded samples (Mackie et al. 2018), with minor spectrometer versions. MS parameters were set as follows: MS1 Ã¢â‚¬â€œ 120,000 resolution at  200 over the m/z range 350Ã¢â‚¬â€œ1400, AGC target of 300, maximum injection time (IT) of 25  MS2 Ã¢â‚¬â€œ top 10 mode, 60,000 resolution, AGC target of 200, maximum IT of 118 ms, HCD collision energy of 30% and dynamic exclusion of 20 s.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Bioinformatic analyses of raw data obtained from LC-MS/MS runs were performed using PEAKS Studio X software. We created a comprehensive Molluscan protein database using sequences present in public databases and publications. The database included: 1) Molluscan proteins downloaded directly from NCBI and 2) proteins translated from transcriptomes. The latter were obtained from sources: a) by downloading sequences from the TSA (translated shotgun assemblies) repository based in NCBI, b) de novo assembled mantle transcriptome for Tridacna crocea recently published (Takeuchi et al. 2021) and thus obtained through this publication; c) de novo mantle transcriptome for Spondylus gaederopus which has been extracted and assembled by our group (Sakalauskaite et al., in preparation). In detail: 1) Molluscan protein NCBI dataset was created by downloading all of the sequences from NCBI protein repository, restricting the taxonomy to phylum Mollusca and selecting the classes of bivalves, gastropods and tusk shells. Sequences were downloaded on 08.03.2021 and included in total 297891 sequences (including common laboratory contaminant proteins). 2). TSA sequences (assembled transcriptomes) were downloaded from NCBI sequence set browser restricting the taxonomy to Mollusca phylum and belonging to either gastropod or bivalve class (no transcriptome projects were present for tusk shells, download date 13.04.2021). The sequencing projects were further filtered to retain those where RNA was isolated either from the mantle or the whole body (i.e., transcripts that have the highest likelihood to be related to shell production). The downloaded sequences were combined and further processed on UseGalaxy Europe platform (Galaxy Community 2022). We also added two de novo assembled molluscan mantle transcriptomes from Tridacna crocea (Takeuchi et al. 2021) and Spondylus gaederopus. The RNA data were translated to protein sequences using the TransDecoder tool which finds coding regions within transcripts. The output fasta files were renamed (to include species name on each protein sequence) and combined to one large protein dataset from transcripts. This accounted for a total of 1463816 sequences from 41 different molluscan species. Finally, all of the sequences were combined together; this Ã¢â‚¬Å“Molluscan Protein databaseÃ¢â‚¬ï¿½ includes a total of 1761707 sequences.  Peptide and protein identification Product ion spectra obtained via Peaks Studio X software were searched against the Molluscan Protein database described above. Search parameters were defined with semi specific tryptic digestion, fragment ion mass tolerance 0.05 Da, and parent ion tolerance of 20 ppm. For peptide identification and protein match, we used the results obtained by SPIDER searches (including all possible modifications). Threshold values for the acceptance of high quality peptides: false discovery rate (FDR) 0.5%, protein scores Ã¢Ë†â€™10lgP Ã¢â€°Â¥ 30, unique peptides Ã¢â€°Â¥ 1, de novo sequences scores (ALC %) Ã¢â€°Â¥ 50. All of the identified protein sequences were checked and assessed manually. Proteins were excluded from further analyses in cases where: a) they were supported only by peptides with low complexity domains (LCDs). These domains are a common feature of many shell proteins (Sakalauskaite et al. 2019) and are not taxon specific; b) supporting peptides and protein showed homology to contaminant sequences (e.g. trypsin); c) they were also found in blank samples and thus, further considered as contamination.

Characteristics[Specimen]: Over 2,500 beads were found on the chest and neck
Characteristics[NumberOfSamples]: the “group of Stuttgart” (GS), composed from 888 items (35%)
the “group of Nice” (GN) totalling of 1697 relatively well-preserved items (65%)
Microscopic traces related to the manufacturing and use-wear processes were recorded for a total of 440 items (25.92%)
